kept.text:
$SOFI EARNINGS: 6 KEY POINTS THAT MATTER 

We’re approaching the moment where conviction meets reality. Risk comes from not knowing what you’re doing. Look out for the following : 

1) WATCH FOR LPB POSITIONING

I'll be watching to see if management makes it clear that LPB is their main, scalable, capital-light growth engine. Investors don't want to hear it as a small channel. Find out how the $3.6B+ new partnership pipeline works and how it brings in a lot of high-margin fees.

2) FEE-BASED NARRATIVE SHIFT

A lot of stress will be on how quickly the shift to a capital-light, fee heavy revenue model is happening. Expect them to talk about how record fee revenue is growing (already about $1.8B a year) and how it makes the balance sheet less intense and the interest rate less sensitive. This is the story of the valuation rerating.

3) CREDIT QUALITY

Clear confidence in underwriting, loss assumptions and personal loan metrics. Watch out for stable delinquency/charge-off trends, rebuttal of short-seller concerns and any forward tone on resilience in a higher-for-longer rate environment. Stability here kills the bear case.

4) FORWARD GUIDANCE TONE

How confident and focused on growth is the commentary? Listen for any small increases to the full year 2026 goals (revenue of about $4.655B and EBITDA of about $1.6B) or positive news about a growth rate of 30% or more. A bullish tone could bring relief after a lot of ups and downs.

5) SHORT SELLER RESPONSE

A direct, confident and data backed defense of the honesty of accounting, the strength of the balance sheet and the overall openness of the company. Expect them to back up the "factually inaccurate" view of Muddy Waters and point out any legal or next step language. This builds trust in institutions.

6) DEPOSIT GROWTH DURABILITY

Confirm that deposit momentum is still strong (aiming for about $40 billion or more), which keeps funding costs low, supports NIM growth and powers the low cost flywheel. This is the basis for everything else.

kept.text:
$SOFI (Algo Flow) - 🔴 Bearish

Last week, options flow crossed below zero on day one and never looked back. By Friday it was accelerating to -1.2M while price slid from $19.75 down to $18. Flow and price moved together the entire week.

What the big money is telling us: Net options activity in SOFI was bearish all week and picked up speed into the close.  

Earnings hit Tuesday April 29 pre-market. Analyst consensus sits at $20.50 median — but a miss or guidance cut could test $17. A beat could push $22-25. 

Floor to watch: $17.50. Until then, flow is in charge.

quoted_tweet.text:
$SOFI (Max Pain $18 | Price $18) — May 1 expiry

2.3M in calls at $35 and price is sitting exactly at the pin. Both sides are loaded.

Puts run heavy from $2 all the way through $13, over 1.5M stacked at the low strikes. Calls stay quiet through $20, build from $22, then accelerate into $35 where the peak hits 2.3M. 

Price at max pain means dealers are near neutral right now, so the next move decides everything. Hold $18 and the call ramp opens up. Slip below and 1.5M in puts drag it down fast.

🎯 Loaded at the pin. $18 holds or it doesn't. $22 is where the call side wakes up, $35 is the ceiling. 

Which side wins this week?

kept.text:
$sofi

The one thing this chart does show, which applies to both Sofi and Lending Club, is that no matter what the fv initial calculations... over the term of the loan all that premium gets amortized against income so at the end of the term, the total income from the premiums is zero.

Now you can say but the higher premiums do boost earnings earlier and by a higher amount... but that happens the first year (and , is appropriate for the difference in loan quality and defaults at fifteenth fico scores etc). 

However,  if both companies do this for multiple years. In the fifth year, they both add premiums for new loans for that year but those premiums are offset by amortization from loans issued in each of the 5 prior years (assuming a 5 year loan) so that the net fair value gain for that year is entirely eliminated in both companies models. 

So after several years, it's simply irrelevant except for the growth in loan originations,  which will also be offset. 

The rain this works and is appropriate under gaap is because if you are in the business of selling loans but hold some loans, the loans you hold should be valued roughly in comparison to the loan sales under the matching principle. 

As you hold loans,  you get paid back primcipal but you lose the ability to obtain premiums from the sales.

It's actually a model that better tracks the economics than cecl.
I know a company that has never double missed in the fintech space. 👀 $SOFI https://t.co/B2109U6j6K
